title: Troubleshooting Emotion Regulation Skills: When What You Are Doing Isn’t Working 1
source_marker: Emotion Regulation Handout 24 (Emotion Regulation Worksheet 16) (p. 1 of 2)

pages.text:
266
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Emotion Regulation Handout 24 (Emotion Regulation Worksheet 16) (p. 1 of 2)
Troubleshooting Emotion Regulation Skills: When What
You Are Doing Isn’t Working
1
Check Your Biological Sensitivity
•• Ask: Am I biologically more vulnerable?
Do I have untreated physical illness or distress?
Am I out of balance on eating, use of drugs, sleep, exercise?
Have I taken medications as prescribed?
•• Work on your PLEASE skills.
1. Take care of physical illness and distress.
2. Take medications as prescribed. Check if others are needed.
3. Try again.
2
Check Your Skills
•• Review what you have tried.
Did you try a skill likely to be effective?
Did you follow the skill instructions to the letter?
•• Work on your skills.
1. Review and try other skills.
2. Get coaching if you need it.
3. Try again.
3
Check for Reinforcers
•• Ask: Do my emotions...
Communicate an important message or influence people to do things?
Motivate me to do things I think are important?
Validate my beliefs or my identity?
Feel good?
•• If yes:
1. Practice interpersonal effectiveness skills to communicate.
2. Work to find new reinforcers to motivate yourself.
3. Practice self-validation.
4. Do pros and cons for changing emotions.
(See Emotion Regulation Worksheet 1.)
(continued on next page)

267
Emotion Regulation Handout 24 (p. 2 of 2)
4
Check Your Mood
•• Ask: Am I putting in the time and effort that solving my problem will take?
•• If no:
1. Do pros and cons for working hard on skills.
2. Practice radical acceptance and willingness skills.
3. Practice the mindfulness skills of participating and effectiveness
(See Mindfulness Handouts 4 and 5.)
5
Check for Emotional Overload
•• Ask: Am I too upset to use complicated skills?
•• If yes, ask: Can the problems I am worrying about be easily solved now?
•• If yes, do problem solving.
(See Emotion Regulation Handouts 9, 12.)
•• If no, practice mindfulness of current emotions.
(See Emotion Regulation Handout 22.)
•• If your emotions are too high for you to think straight:
•• Go to TIP skills.
(See Distress Tolerance Handout 5.)
6
Check for Emotion Myths Getting in the Way
•• Check for:
Judgmental myths about emotions (e.g., “Some emotions are stupid,” “There is a
right way to feel in every situation”)?
Beliefs that emotions and identity are the same (e.g., “My emotions are who I
am”)?
•• If yes:
1. Check the facts.
2. Challenge myths.
3. Practice thinking nonjudgmentally.

full_text:
Emotion Regulation Handout 24 (Emotion Regulation Worksheet 16) (p. 1 of 2)
Troubleshooting Emotion Regulation Skills: When What
You Are Doing Isn’t Working
Check Your Biological Sensitivity
•• Ask: Am I biologically more vulnerable?
Do I have untreated physical illness or distress?
Am I out of balance on eating, use of drugs, sleep, exercise?
Have I taken medications as prescribed?
•• Work on your PLEASE skills.
1. Take care of physical illness and distress.
2. Take medications as prescribed. Check if others are needed.
3. Try again.
Check Your Skills
•• Review what you have tried.
Did you try a skill likely to be effective?
Did you follow the skill instructions to the letter?
•• Work on your skills.
1. Review and try other skills.
2. Get coaching if you need it.
3. Try again.
Check for Reinforcers
•• Ask: Do my emotions...
Communicate an important message or influence people to do things?
Motivate me to do things I think are important?
Validate my beliefs or my identity?
Feel good?
•• If yes:
1. Practice interpersonal effectiveness skills to communicate.
2. Work to find new reinforcers to motivate yourself.
3. Practice self-validation.
4. Do pros and cons for changing emotions.
(See Emotion Regulation Worksheet 1.)
(continued on next page) Emotion Regulation Handout 24 (p. 2 of 2)
Check Your Mood
•• Ask: Am I putting in the time and effort that solving my problem will take?
•• If no:
1. Do pros and cons for working hard on skills.
2. Practice radical acceptance and willingness skills.
3. Practice the mindfulness skills of participating and effectiveness
(See Mindfulness Handouts 4 and 5.)
Check for Emotional Overload
•• Ask: Am I too upset to use complicated skills?
•• If yes, ask: Can the problems I am worrying about be easily solved now?
•• If yes, do problem solving.
(See Emotion Regulation Handouts 9, 12.)
•• If no, practice mindfulness of current emotions.
(See Emotion Regulation Handout 22.)
•• If your emotions are too high for you to think straight:
•• Go to TIP skills.
(See Distress Tolerance Handout 5.)
Check for Emotion Myths Getting in the Way
•• Check for:
Judgmental myths about emotions (e.g., “Some emotions are stupid,” “There is a
right way to feel in every situation”)?
Beliefs that emotions and identity are the same (e.g., “My emotions are who I
am”)?
•• If yes:
1. Check the facts.
2. Challenge myths.
3. Practice thinking nonjudgmentally.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Troubleshooting Emotion Regulation Skills: When What You Are Doing Isn’t Working 1

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
